title: Dimorphosciadium gayoides
taxonomy: Dimorphosciadium gayoides is classified as a species of flowering plant in the carrot family (Apiaceae). It is native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico. The plant belongs to the genus Dimorphosciadium, which is a small group of plants closely related to the genus Scalica. The exact classification of Dimorphosciadium gayoides may vary depending on the source and any recent reclassifications. More research is needed to confirm the exact classification of this plant.

side_effects: Dimorphosciadium gayoides is a plant native to the southwestern United States and Mexico, and it has been reported to cause skin irritation in some individuals who come into contact with it. intro: Dimorphosciadium gayoides, also known as Gay's cicely or wild angelica, is a perennial plant belonging to the carrot family (Apiaceae). It has been traditionally used in herbal medicine to treat a variety of health issues, including digestive problems, inflammation, and respiratory issues, due to its potential anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and expectorant properties. The bioactive constituents of Dimorphosciadium gayoides include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The plant is commonly used to make herbal teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to treat respiratory issues, such as bronchitis and asthma.
